title: Conditional probability and rare events
intro: Randomness does not only deceive on small samples. There is a trickier trap: even a very accurate test for a rare disease produces a flood of false alarms. Let's see why — this is conditional probability.

beats.text: Take a disease that affects 1 person in 100, and a test that is 90% accurate. The test comes back positive for some people. The bar shows everyone whose test fired: teal — actually sick, yellow — healthy people with a false alarm.
The key question: if your test is positive — what is the probability you are actually sick?
beats.predict: The test is 90% accurate. So the probability of being sick given a positive test is also about 90%?
beats.reveal: No — only about 8%! The sick are few (10 in 1000) and the healthy are many (990), and even a 10% error rate on the huge healthy group yields ~99 false alarms — far more than the 9 real cases. The rarity of the disease outweighs the accuracy of the test.

beats.text: Drag the "disease prevalence" slider. The rarer the disease, the more the false alarms drown out the real cases. Make the disease common — and a positive test is now almost always right. Same test, different meaning of the result.

That is the essence of conditional probability: "the probability of being sick given a positive test" is not the same as "the probability of a positive test given the disease". They get confused constantly. Bayes' theorem connects them, and the key to the answer is the base rate — how rare the event is.

First the notation itself, or the formula looks scary. P(…) means "the probability of …". The vertical bar "|" reads "given". So P(sick | test +) is "the probability of being sick GIVEN a positive test". It is not the same as P(test + | sick) — the reverse: the probability of a positive test in someone who is already sick. Bayes' theorem is just a careful way to compute the same picture: P(sick | test +) = true alarms / all alarms. In our numbers that is 9 true out of (9 + 99) ≈ 8%. Under the chart sits the formula with live numbers: drag "prevalence" and "accuracy" and watch the result change. No magic — we divide the teal part of the bar by the whole bar.

The same logic works outside medicine. Fraud detection: fraud is rare (say 0.2% of transactions) and the detector is "95% accurate". On a million honest transactions, 5% errors means 50,000 false blocks against a handful of real ones. That is why a bank does not block on a single alarm but asks for confirmation: the cost of a false alarm (blocking an honest customer) is high too.

definitions.text: the probability of an event given that another is known. P(sick | test +) is not the same as P(test + | sick).
how common the event is in general, before any test. Rarity drastically changes what a positive result means.
how to update the probability of A after observing B, accounting for both the test's accuracy and the base rate P(A).

practice: This trap is real in medicine: when screening for rare diseases, most positive results turn out false, so the first test is followed by a confirmatory, more precise one.
The same effect drives fraud and spam filters: if fraud is rare, even a good detector will bury you in false alarms unless the base rate is taken into account.
realLife: Breathalyzers, lie detectors, "suspicious person" recognition in crowds — all suffer from this: with a rare event and good accuracy, the absolute number of false alarms is huge.
A competent conclusion therefore always weighs not only "how accurate is the test" but also "how rare is the event itself".
